stem: A newborn has asymmetric constriction rings around several digits with distal edema. Some digits show amputation. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Amniotic band syndrome results from rupture of the amnion, creating fibrous bands that entangle fetal parts. It causes asymmetric, variable defects: constriction rings, limb/digit amputation, syndactyly, clubfoot, and craniofacial clefts (typically atypical — not following normal developmental lines). The key distinguishing feature is asymmetry and random distribution, unlike genetic syndromes which are symmetric/patterned. [StatPearls NBK525986]

feedback.correct: Correct! Asymmetric constriction rings + amputations = amniotic band syndrome from ruptured amnion.
feedback.incorrect: Amniotic bands cause random, asymmetric constrictions and amputations — unlike symmetric genetic conditions.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: What distinguishes amniotic band-related craniofacial clefts from typical cleft lip/palate?

choices.text: They are atypical, not following normal developmental lines

explanation: Amniotic band craniofacial clefts are atypical — they do not follow embryologic fusion planes. Typical cleft lip/palate occurs along the line of failed fusion of the maxillary and medial nasal prominences. Amniotic band clefts can occur at any location, are often oblique or asymmetric, and may involve encephaloceles. This distinction is critical for surgical planning and genetic counseling. [StatPearls NBK525986]

feedback.correct: Correct! Amniotic band clefts are atypical and random, not following embryologic fusion lines.
feedback.incorrect: Typical clefts follow fusion planes. Amniotic band clefts are atypical, random, and often asymmetric.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A fetus on ultrasound has limb constrictions and abnormal positioning not consistent with any genetic syndrome. What is the suspected etiology?

choices.text: Amniotic band syndrome from amnion rupture

explanation: Amniotic band syndrome occurs from early rupture of the amnion. The fetus then enters the chorionic cavity where fibrous bands (from the amnion) can entangle limbs and body parts. It is sporadic (not genetic), not associated with chromosomal abnormalities, and has a recurrence risk near zero. Severity depends on timing of rupture and location of band entanglement. [StatPearls NBK525986]

feedback.correct: Correct! Sporadic amnion rupture → fibrous bands → random constrictions. Not genetic, near-zero recurrence.
feedback.incorrect: Amniotic band syndrome is sporadic from amnion rupture. Not chromosomal, not inherited.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 2-month-old infant has asymmetric thigh skin folds and limited hip abduction on the left. Ortolani test produces a palpable 'clunk.' What is the diagnosis?
explanation: Positive Ortolani test (relocating a dislocated hip with a clunk) and Barlow test (dislocating an unstable hip) are the hallmark physical exam findings for DDH in neonates. Risk factors: female sex, breech presentation, firstborn, family history. Screening ultrasound recommended at 4-6 weeks for high-risk infants. Treatment: Pavlik harness before 6 months; surgical reduction if older. [StatPearls NBK441981]

feedback.correct: Correct! Ortolani clunk = relocating a dislocated hip = DDH. Pavlik harness before 6 months.
feedback.incorrect: Asymmetric folds + limited abduction + positive Ortolani = DDH. Screen high-risk infants with ultrasound.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: What is the preferred imaging modality for diagnosing DDH in an infant younger than 4-6 months?
explanation: Hip ultrasound is the preferred imaging for DDH in infants <4-6 months because the femoral head is cartilaginous and not visible on X-ray. After 4-6 months, when ossification has progressed, pelvic X-ray becomes useful. The Pavlik harness is first-line treatment for infants <6 months, maintaining the hip in flexion and abduction. Success rate ~95% when initiated early. [StatPearls NBK441981]

feedback.correct: Correct! Ultrasound is used <6 months (cartilaginous head). X-ray after ossification at 4-6 months.
feedback.incorrect: Femoral head is cartilage in young infants → ultrasound needed. X-ray only useful after ossification begins.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: Which risk factor is MOST strongly associated with developmental dysplasia of the hip?
explanation: Breech presentation is the strongest risk factor for DDH, particularly frank breech (hips flexed, knees extended). The sustained position of hip flexion/adduction limits normal acetabular development. Other risk factors: female (4:1), firstborn (tight uterine musculature), family history, and oligohydramnios. Left hip is more commonly affected (left occiput anterior position compresses the left hip against the sacrum). [StatPearls NBK441981]

feedback.correct: Correct! Breech presentation is the strongest risk factor. Frank breech limits normal acetabular development.
feedback.incorrect: Breech = strongest DDH risk factor. Also: female sex, firstborn, family history, left hip > right.

auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use
